1: gibbôr ḥayil (H1368 + H2428) — “mighty warrior/man of valor” — the same honorific used of Gideon (6:12) and Boaz (Ruth 2:1). The juxtaposition with bên-ʾiššāh zônāh (son of a prostitute, H802) is abrupt: the text establishes Jephthah’s military credential before naming his social liability. Gilʿādî (the Gileadite) at the end of the verse completes the triple identification — Jephthah is a Gileadite warrior of prowess whose birth excludes him from the inheritance he should have had.
2: naḥălāh (H5159, inheritance) governs the conflict: Gilead’s legitimate sons deny Jephthah his portion. The Hebrew phrase lōʾ-tinḥal bəḇêt-ʾāḇînû — “you will not inherit in our father’s house” — is a formal legal exclusion. Israelite inheritance law tied naḥălāh to the status of the mother; a son of a slave-woman or prostitute could be excluded if the father’s legitimate heirs objected (cf. Ishmael, Gen 21:10).
3: ʾănāšîm rêqîm (H376 + H7386, empty/worthless men) — the same phrase used for Abimelech’s retinue (9:4). The parallel is deliberate: like Abimelech, Jephthah attracts a marginal following when legitimacy is denied. Tôḇ as a place-name (location uncertain, perhaps in northern Transjordan) means “good” — an ironic settlement name for a displaced outcast. wayyēṣēʾ ʾittô — they raided with him — establishes his career as a mercenary warlord before his commission.
4: Narrative transition: miyyāmîm (H3117, after some time/days) — the formula marks an unspecified lapse before the Ammonite crisis. The elders’ need reverses the power dynamic established in v2; the rejected son becomes the only viable military leader.
5: The elders of Gilead make the journey to retrieve Jephthah — a reversal that the narrator frames as driven by necessity (wayyāqumû, they rose up). The verb lāqaḥ (H3947, to take/fetch) acknowledges they are bringing him back on his terms, not theirs.
6: qāṣîn (H7101, commander/ruler) — the elders’ initial offer. The term is military and functional rather than covenantal; it offers command authority without social rehabilitation. Jephthah’s counter-negotiation in vv.8-11 upgrades this to rōʾš (head/chief), securing a permanent civic position alongside the military role.
7: Jephthah’s direct challenge (lāmmāh, why?) rehearses the injustice: śənēʾtem (you hated me, H8130) and gāraštem (you expelled me, H1644). Both verbs are perfects — completed acts Jephthah refuses to overlook. His return is not unconditional; he negotiates from grievance rather than gratitude.
8: The elders’ counter-offer expands: now rōʾš (H7218, head/chief) over all the inhabitants of Gilead, not merely military commander. The political elevation is the concession Jephthah required; it transforms a military commission into a permanent civil authority.
9: Jephthah frames the arrangement as conditional on YHWH’s action: ʾim-YHWH yittên ʾet-bənê ʿammôn — the victory is divine gift, not Jephthah’s personal achievement. This theological frame will re-appear in the vow (v30) and in the argument to the Ammonite king (vv.21-23): YHWH is the real warrior and judge.
10: YHWH yihyeh šōmēaʿ bênênû — “YHWH shall be the one hearing between us” — covenant-witness language. šōmēaʿ (H8085, hearing/witnessing) frames YHWH as the enforcer of the agreement; violation becomes not merely breach of promise but sin against YHWH. The verbal covenant sealed at Mizpah (v11) is thus given divine sanction.
11: rōʾš wəqāṣîn — the dual title ratifies both civic and military authority. waydabbēr Yiptāḥ ʾet-kol-dəḇārāyw lipnê YHWH bammizpāh — “Jephthah spoke all his words before YHWH at Mizpah” — the covenant terms are stated in YHWH’s presence, constituting a formal oath before the divine witness invoked in v10.
12: Diplomatic protocol: Jephthah sends messengers (malʾāḵîm) rather than attacking immediately — unusual for judges, who typically respond with force. This extended diplomatic exchange (vv.12-28) showcases Jephthah as a legal reasoner who argues Israel’s case from historical precedent before resorting to war.
13: The Ammonite claim: Israel took their land when coming from Egypt — specifically the territory from the Arnon to the Jabbok and Heshbon. Historically, this was Amorite territory (Sihon’s kingdom) that Israel captured in Num 21, not Ammonite land; Jephthah’s response will press exactly this distinction.
14: Second embassy — the repetition of messengers (šûḇ, again) emphasizes Jephthah’s exhaustion of diplomatic channels before armed conflict. The legal argument in vv.15-27 is the fullest diplomatic speech in Judges.
15: Jephthah’s denial is categorical: lōʾ-lāqaḥ Yiśrāʾēl ʾet-ʾereṣ Môʾāḇ wəʾet ʾereṣ bənê ʿammôn — “Israel did not take the land of Moab or the land of the Ammonites.” The legal strategy is to distinguish between Moabite/Ammonite territory (which Israel bypassed per Num 20-21) and Amorite territory (which Israel conquered from Sihon).
16: The wilderness itinerary recapitulates Num 20-21: Israel went through the desert from Egypt to the Red Sea and then to Kadesh — respecting the borders of Edom and Moab throughout. This is the foundational claim of the legal brief: Israel’s route of travel did not encroach on Ammonite land.
17: The request to pass through (naʿbərāh-nāʾ bəʾarṣeḵā) was refused by the king of Edom; a parallel request to Moab was also refused — but neither Edom nor Moab went to war over Israel’s presence. Jephthah uses the precedent to distinguish refusal to yield passage (which Israel accepted) from active military contest (which only Sihon initiated).
18: The wilderness detour around Edom and Moab is mapped precisely: past Edom, past Moab, arriving on the eastern side of the Arnon — always outside the Ammonite border. The geographical specificity grounds the legal argument; Jephthah knows the itinerary texts of the Pentateuch.
19: The Sihon embassy (šəlaḥ Yiśrāʾēl malʾāḵîm ʾel-Sîḥôn, cf. Num 21:21-22) is identical to the earlier requests: a request for passage, not conquest. The distinction matters legally — Israel’s intent was transit, not territorial seizure; Sihon transformed the encounter into a war by refusing and attacking.
20: wəlōʾ-hehĕʾmîn Sîḥôn — “Sihon did not trust Israel to pass through his territory.” hehĕʾmîn (H539 Hiphil, to believe/trust) is the verbal root of ʾĕmet (faithfulness); Sihon’s failure of trust provoked the war. This reframes the conquest as defensive rather than aggressive on Israel’s part.
21: wayyittên YHWH ʾĕlōhê Yiśrāʾēl ʾet-Sîḥôn wəʾet-kol-ʿammô — “YHWH the God of Israel gave Sihon and all his people into the hand of Israel.” The passive recipient of Amorite territory is YHWH’s gift to Israel, not Israel’s independent seizure — the legal and theological crux of the entire argument.
22: The territorial scope of the gift: from the Arnon to the Jabbok, from the wilderness to the Jordan. These are the same boundaries the Ammonite king claims in v13. Jephthah establishes that this was Amorite land (Sihon’s), not Ammonite, making the Ammonite territorial claim false on its own historical terms.
23: The theological summation: YHWH ʾĕlōhê Yiśrāʾēl hôrîš ʾet-hāʾĕmōrî — “YHWH the God of Israel dispossessed the Amorites.” hôrîš (H3423 Hiphil, to dispossess/drive out) places the action entirely with YHWH; the rhetorical question follows: “Are you to possess what YHWH our God has given us?”
24: The Chemosh argument: ʾăšer yôrîšeḵā Kəmôš ʾĕlōhêḵā — “whatever Chemosh your god gives you to possess.” This is a ius gentium (law of nations) argument, not a concession that Chemosh is real: Jephthah uses the Ammonite theology to make a parallel legal claim. The logic is “by your own principles, territorial possession follows divine grant.” This does not imply Israelite polytheism.
25: The Balak precedent (Bālāq ben-Ṣippôr) adds a historical-legal argument: even the most hostile Moabite king did not go to war to recover the territory Israel possessed for 300 years. Long non-contestation equals legal acquiescence in ancient near-eastern custom.
26: The 300-year possession claim (šəlōš meʾôt šānāh) asserts prescriptive title: Israel occupied Heshbon and the Transjordanian towns for three centuries without Ammonite challenge. The span corresponds roughly to the period from the conquest to Jephthah’s era. The rhetorical question “why did you not recover them in that time?” presses the laches argument.
27: YHWH haššōpēṭ — “YHWH the Judge” — Jephthah’s final appeal elevates the dispute to divine jurisdiction. šōpēṭ (H8199) is the same root as the term for Israel’s judges; YHWH is the supreme judge over inter-national disputes. Jephthah has exhausted legal argument; he submits the outcome to divine verdict.
28: wəlōʾ šāmaʿ melek bənê ʿammôn — “the king of the Ammonites did not listen.” The stark brevity after Jephthah’s elaborate legal argument marks the failure of diplomacy. šāmaʿ (H8085, to hear/heed) is the same verb used for YHWH’s attentiveness in v10; the Ammonite king’s deafness contrasts with YHWH’s role as the hearing witness.
29: rûaḥ YHWH hāyətāh ʿal-Yiptāḥ — “the Spirit of the LORD came upon Jephthah.” The identical construction appears at Othniel (3:10), Gideon (6:34), and Samson (13:25, 14:6, 19); the Spirit’s ḥāyāh ʿal (being upon someone) is the divine empowerment pattern for Israel’s deliverers. Jephthah’s subsequent military success is attributed to the Spirit, not his warrior skill alone.
30: wayyiddār Yiptāḥ neder laYHWH — “Jephthah vowed a vow to the LORD.” nādar neder (H5087 + H5088) is a cognate-object construction that intensifies the solemnity: not simply “he vowed” but “he vowed a vow” — comparable to “he dreamed a dream” or “he sinned a sin.” The vow is conditional on military victory: ʾim-nātôn tittēn (if you will indeed give) uses the infinitive absolute to signal certainty of intention.
31: The vow’s terms: hayyôṣēʾ ʾăšer yēṣēʾ middalṯê ḇêtî — “whatever goes out from the doors of my house.” The Hebrew has two consecutive clauses: wəhāyāh laYHWH (“it shall be the LORD’s”) and weheʿĕlîtîhû ʿōlāh (“and I will offer it as a burnt offering”). Whether the second waw is conjunctive (and) or disjunctive (or) determines whether Jephthah envisions one action (consecration-then-sacrifice) or two alternatives (if an animal, burnt offering; if a person, dedicated to YHWH). The text preserves the ambiguity without resolution.
32: wayyittēn YHWH ʾôtām bəyādô — “YHWH gave them into his hand.” The victory formula identical to the judge-cycle pattern; YHWH’s agency is foregrounded over Jephthah’s military action. The brief narrative of battle (v32-33) stands in contrast to the elaborate diplomatic speech; the actual war is almost incidental.
33: The victory geography: from Aroer to Minnith (20 cities) and Abel-keramim — a broad sweep across Transjordanian territory. makkāh gədôlāh məʾōd (a very great blow) is the deuteronomistic superlative indicating complete defeat. The Ammonite claim of v13 is answered by military outcome rather than legal verdict.
34: bittô yəḥîdāh lô — “she was his only child (yāḥîd).” yāḥîd (H3173, only/unique) is the same word used for Isaac in Gen 22:2 (your only son, whom you love). The echo is likely deliberate: the father who offered his only child, the child who accepted the sacrifice. tuppîm ûmāḥôlôt (tambourines and dancing) mark the homecoming celebration; the daughter comes to greet the returning warrior, the very scenario the vow implied.
35: wayyiqraʿ bigādāyw — “he tore his clothes” (mourning gesture). ʿāḵôr ʿăḵartinî — “you have brought me very low” — lit. “you have troubled/bent me.” pāṣîtî pî ʾel-YHWH — “I have opened my mouth to the LORD” — acknowledges the binding force of the vow; lōʾ ʾûḵal lāšûḇ — “I cannot take it back.” Jephthah treats the vow as irrevocable, which accords with Num 30:2 (a man must not break his word).
36: The daughter’s response demonstrates extraordinary covenantal integrity: ʿăśēh-lî kāʾăšer yāṣāʾ mippiḵā — “do to me according to what has gone out of your mouth.” She grounds her acceptance in the premise that YHWH has avenged Jephthah against his enemies; the vow and its fulfillment are inseparable from YHWH’s role as the one who gave the victory. Her language echoes the legal framework of the entire chapter.
37: The daughter’s request: two months to wander with her companions on the mountains and weep over her bəṯûlîm (H1331, virginity). The word choice is significant: she mourns her virginity/youth, not her impending death. If Jephthah intended human sacrifice, weeping over barrenness is an unusual focus; the text’s emphasis on virginity fits the consecration-to-lifelong-celibacy interpretation.
38: Permission granted; she goes for two months with her companions. wattēleḵ hîʾ wərêʿôtêhā watteḇke ʿal-bəṯûlêhā — the mourning is communal (with her companions) and specifically for her virginity, not for loss of life. The narrator’s precision here signals what is grieved.
39: wayyaʿaś lāh ʾet-nidərô ʾăšer nāḏār — “he did to her his vow which he had vowed.” The outcome statement is followed immediately by the clause wəhîʾ lōʾ yādəʿāh ʾîš — “and she had not known a man.” This parenthetical notation is the text’s interpretive key: the narrator marks her permanent virginity as the defining outcome, not her death. Had she been killed, the virginity clause would be irrelevant to the narrative. The execution of the vow is described with ʿāśāh (he did, H6213), which for a burnt offering would normally use ʿālāh or šāḥaṭ.
40: ḥōq bəYiśrāʾēl (H2706, a statute/custom in Israel) — tānāh (H8567, to commemorate/rehearse/lament) is an unusual verb; the daughters of Israel kept a four-day annual mourning. The word tānāh appears only here in the Hebrew Bible, possibly meaning “to rehearse in memory.” The custom memorializes specifically Jephthah’s daughter, not Jephthah — a mark of the narrator’s sympathy for the victim of the vow.
